title: Equal Residues After Increments
description:
### Objective
You may add k to any subset of elements any number of times. Determine whether all numbers can be made equal. This reduces to checking whether all elements share the same remainder modulo k. Return 1 if possible, else 0. If k is zero, equality is only possible if all numbers are the same already.

### Function Contract
Implement `equal_pairs_after_increments(nums: List[int], k: int)` in Python.
Do not change input/output semantics expected by the tests.

### Input and Output
- Input `nums`: type `array` (example: `[2,4,6]`).
- Input `k`: type `number` (example: `2`).
- Return type: `number`.

### Requirements
- Return the exact expected output for every test case.
- Handle edge cases (empty input, duplicates, boundary values) without raising exceptions.
- Target complexity: time `O(n)`, space `O(1)`.

### Constraints
- 1 ≤ len(nums) ≤ 10^5
- -10^9 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^9
- 0 ≤ k ≤ 10^9
explanation:
Intuition
Adding k repeatedly preserves the value modulo k. If all numbers share the same remainder r under modulo k, we can reach a common value by raising smaller residues by multiples of k. When k is zero, increments are impossible so initial equality is required.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: If k == 0, test whether all values are identical.
2. Main Algorithm: Compute each value modulo k and ensure all residues match the first residue.
3. Return Result: Return 1 if they match, otherwise 0.

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n) because the algorithm scans elements a constant
  number of times and performs O(1) work for each update or lookup.
- Space Complexity: O(1) due to the auxiliary structures required to
  store counts, prefix sums, or membership sets while ensuring fast access.

Key Insights
- Invariance under modulo explains reachability after repeated adds
- Normalization simplifies many transformation problems

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting the k == 0 special case
- Using negative mods inconsistently in some languages

testCases.explanation: Cannot change values when k is zero

hints: What property remains unchanged when adding k repeatedly
Handle k == 0 as a special case
